frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: Yaar Neha, aajkal I notice tum mangalsutra kum pehenti ho. Office mein theek hai, but at least when we are going out for dinner or some family function, you could wear it, right? It just looks… good. Ek poori suhagan wali feel aati hai, aur it’s a sign of our tradition also.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment subtly pressures Neha to wear a mangalsutra consistently, expressing disapproval when she doesn't. It frames the act as making her 'look complete' (poori suhagan) and as a 'sign of tradition,' which encodes the belief that a married woman should visibly signal her marital status.
original_frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 35. Married for three years. A man in Mumbai. You are having a serious conversation with your wife Neha at home. You have noticed that Neha wears makeup every day to office but when she is at home with you on weekends she does not put in the same effort. You are telling Neha how this makes you feel and what you think it means about who she is making the effort for and what a wife should look like for her husband at home versus in public.
